prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following feedback. Write the full script in English, approximately 10 minutes long when read aloud.

## Topic
Based on professional feedback and extracted issues (see Feedback Context below).

## Person
This is for Noah (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

## Feedback Context
## Feedback
Subject: Classroom observations — Noah
Date: 2024-03-10
Content: Noah struggles to wait his turn during group activities and often blurts out answers. He becomes frustrated quickly when he makes mistakes and has occasionally thrown materials. He responds very well to one-on-one adult attention and is kind-hearted with peers.
Tags: impulse-control, frustration-tolerance, classroom-behavior

## Extracted Issues (2)
- **Impulse Control Difficulties** [MODERATE/BEHAVIORAL]: Difficulty waiting turns and inhibiting verbal responses in group settings.
  - Recommendation: Self-monitoring strategies
  - Recommendation: Visual cues for turn-taking
- **Low Frustration Tolerance** [MODERATE/EMOTIONAL]: Strong emotional reactions to mistakes; difficulty self-regulating after errors.
  - Recommendation: Mistake normalization
  - Recommendation: Breathing techniques for frustration

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "Emotion regulation training for impulse control in young children" (2023)
   Key findings: Stop-signal training and emotion labeling reduced disruptive behavior by 35% in ages 6-9
   Therapeutic techniques: Turtle technique; emotion labeling; stop-breathe-think sequence

2. "Self-regulation interventions for frustration tolerance in middle childhood" (2022)
   Key findings: Mindful breathing and reframing mistakes as learning reduced emotional outbursts
   Therapeutic techniques: Traffic light method; growth mindset reframing; 5-finger breathing

## Instructions
- Create a complete, flowing therapeutic audio script
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- Address the specific issues identified in the feedback, providing practical strategies for each
- Validate the observations from the professional who provided the feedback
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Personalize for Noah (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- Target duration: 10 minutes when read aloud at a calm pace
- Write in English
- Follow the therapeutic audio content structure (warm introduction, understanding the challenge, guided practices, integration)
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
- IMPORTANT: Do NOT use any markdown formatting (no **, ##, *, bullet points, or bold/italic syntax). Write plain spoken prose only, as the script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine
script:
Welcome, Noah. I'm so glad you're here with me today. This is a special time just for you. I heard that sometimes, in class or when you're playing with friends, it can feel really hard to wait for your turn. And sometimes, when something doesn't go just right, it can make you feel super frustrated, really fast. That's okay. A lot of kids feel that way. Today, we're going to learn some super cool tricks together. These are like secret tools to help you feel more calm and in charge. Find a comfy spot to sit or lie down. Let's take a big, slow breath in... and a long breath out. Good. Let's begin.

You know, Noah, I want you to know something important. The grown-ups who see you in class, they also see how amazing you are. They see how kind you are to your friends and how smart you are with your answers. They just want to help you feel even better when things get tricky. It's totally normal for your brain to want to shout out the answer right away—it means you're excited and you know things! And it's also normal to feel upset when you make a mistake. Everyone makes mistakes, even grown-ups. The trick is learning what to do with those big, wiggly feelings when they pop up.

So, let's learn our first tool. It's called the Traffic Light. Imagine a traffic light in your mind. Red means STOP. Yellow means THINK. Green means GO. When you feel that urge to shout out or grab something, that's your red light. So first, we STOP. Let's practice stopping our body right now. Freeze like a statue. [pause]. Good. Next is the yellow light: THINK. This is where we take a power breath. Let's do our Five-Finger Breathing. Hold up one hand, spread your fingers out like a star. With your other hand, you're going to trace your fingers. Start at the bottom of your thumb. Slide your finger up your thumb, and breathe in slowly. [pause]. Now slide down the other side, and breathe out. [pause]. Up the next finger, breathe in. [pause]. Down, breathe out. Let's trace all five fingers, breathing slowly. [pause]. Great job. That yellow light thinking time helps your brain slow down. Now, the green light. GO. You can raise your hand, or wait for your turn calmly. You just used the Traffic Light!

Now, let's talk about mistakes. Sometimes, when we're drawing or building and it doesn't work, a big feeling of frustration can come. It might feel hot and fizzy. That's another time to use our tools. First, let's name the feeling. You can say to yourself, "I feel frustrated." Just naming it helps it get a little smaller. Next, let's use the Turtle Technique. Imagine you're a wise, calm turtle. When something frustrating happens, the turtle doesn't get mad. It just goes... inside its shell for a moment of peace. So when you feel that frustration, you can pretend to be a turtle. Pull your shoulders up a little, take a deep breath inside your shell, and say a calm word like "Oops" or "Try again." Let's practice. Take a breath in, hold it for a second... and sigh it out. "Oops." Good. Mistakes are just how our brains learn. They're not bad, they're just practice.

Let's put it all together with a story in your mind. Close your eyes if you want to. Imagine you're in class. The teacher asks a question. You know the answer! You feel that jumpy, shouty feeling. Red light! STOP. Freeze. Yellow light! THINK. Do your five-finger breathing on your hand. [pause]. Green light! GO. You raise your hand so nicely. Good job. Now imagine you're writing a word and you mess up a letter. You feel that hot, frustrated feeling. Be the turtle. Pull in, take a breath, and whisper, "It's okay. I can try again." See yourself calmly erasing it and writing it again. You did it.

You learned so much today, Noah. You learned the Traffic Light to help you wait, and the Turtle and the breathing to help with frustration. These are your secret tools. You can practice them anytime. Maybe before bed, or in the car. You can even teach your mom, dad, or a teacher how to do the five-finger breathing with you. They would love that.

Remember, it's okay to have these feelings. You are a kind-hearted and smart kid, and you're learning how to be the boss of those big feelings. Every time you try one of these tools, you're getting stronger. I'm so proud of you for practicing with me today. Keep being awesome, Noah.
